canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, visual grounding is predominantly defined as a model's latent ability to connect textual descriptions or instructions with their corresponding spatial regions in a visual input. While this core concept is widely shared, the specific terminology varies, including "multimodal grounding," "spatial understanding," "phrase grounding," and "referring expression comprehension," applied to diverse visual media such as images, 3D scenes, videos, and GUIs. A much less common usage, found in one paper, frames "rule grounding" as a logical task of instantiating abstract rules with constants. The field most frequently operationalizes this construct through tasks requiring the localization of objects, which are measured by benchmarks like RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and RefCOCOg. The expected model behavior is to produce a spatial reference, such as generating bounding box coordinates or segmentation masks, with one study noting that models are "required to explicitly ground objects in images by generating coordinates for them" (Verifiable by Design: Aligning Language Models to Quote from Pre-Training Data). Visual grounding is often discussed in contrast to hallucination, with multiple papers describing hallucinations as responses that are "plausible yet visually ungrounded" (The Hidden Life of Tokens: Reducing Hallucination of Large Vision-Language Models Via Visual Information Steering). The construct is reported to influence capabilities like robotic manipulation and visual question answering and is studied alongside related tasks such as object detection and semantic segmentation.

definitions.text: The latent ability of a model to associate textual descriptions, such as phrases or referring expressions, with their corresponding spatial regions in an image.
definitions.paper: Grounding Multimodal Large Language Models to the World

definitions.text: The latent ability of a model to understand a visual reference, such as a bounding box, and generate an appropriate textual description for the specified object or region.
definitions.paper: Grounding Multimodal Large Language Models to the World

definitions.text: The ability to use scene information to connect language instructions with visible objects and their states in the environment.
definitions.paper: LoTa-Bench: Benchmarking Language-oriented Task Planners for Embodied Agents

definitions.text: The latent ability to align visual regions with semantic descriptions, enabling both comprehension of spatial references and localization of described content.
definitions.paper: Ferret: Refer and Ground Anything Anywhere at Any Granularity

definitions.text: The task of localizing a specific 3D object in a scene based on a natural language description.
Generating a segmentation mask for a specific object in an image based on a natural language referring expression.
definitions.paper: GeoPixel: Pixel Grounding Large Multimodal Model in Remote Sensing

definitions.text: The alignment of detected shapes in a diagram (e.g., circle, rectangle) with their semantic labels and spatial locations.
definitions.paper: Primitive Vision: Improving Diagram Understanding in MLLMs

definitions.text: Predicting the precise spatio-temporal coordinates (frame, x, y) of a referred object in a video as a tuple output.
definitions.paper: TUMTraf VideoQA: Dataset and Benchmark for Unified Spatio-Temporal Video Understanding in Traffic Scenes

definitions.text: The task of localizing an object or region in an image that is described by a natural language phrase.
The task of associating specific phrases or words in a text description with their corresponding regions or objects in an image.
The model's ability to match textual descriptions with specific image regions, demonstrated through contrastive learning on region-caption pairs.
Producing bounding box coordinates that accurately localize a specified UI element on a mobile screen in response to a textual instruction.
definitions.paper: Improbable Bigrams Expose Vulnerabilities of Incomplete Tokens in Byte-Level Tokenizers

definitions.text: Incorrectly associating textual or visual information from irrelevant regions of the image, leading to inaccurate answers.
definitions.paper: CCQA: Generating Question from Solution Can Improve Inference-Time Reasoning inSLMs

definitions.text: The task of linking a textual description (referring expression) to the corresponding object or region in an image.
definitions.paper: The Role of Outgoing Connection Heterogeneity in Feedforward Layers of Large Language Models

definitions.text: Mapping natural language instructions to specific executable elements or locations on a GUI screen, such as clicking a button or selecting a menu item.
definitions.paper: MoVa: Towards Generalizable Classification of Human Morals and Values

definitions.text: Instantiating abstract rules with specific constants to prepare for logical inference.
definitions.paper: Combining Constrained and Unconstrained Decoding via Boosting:BoostCDand Its Application to Information Extraction

relationships.paper: MoVA: Adapting Mixture of Vision Experts to Multimodal Context
relationships.evidence: To thoroughly assess our model’s understanding of pixel-level instances, we evaluate its performance on referring segmentation and grounding benchmarks, including refCOCO (Kazemzadeh et al., 2014), refCOCO+ (Kazemzadeh et al., 2014), and refCOCOg (Caesar et al., 2018).

relationships.paper: CityEQA: A HierarchicalLLMAgent on Embodied Question Answering Benchmark in City Space
relationships.evidence: Several causes of hallucinations have been identified including biased training data (Liu et al., 2023), the inability of vision encoders to accurately ground images (Jain et al., 2024), misalignment among different modalities (Liu et al., 2024b), and insufficient context attention in LLM decoders (Huang et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024d). (Section 2.2)

characterization: Across the surveyed literature, visual grounding is predominantly defined as a model's latent ability to connect textual descriptions or instructions with their corresponding spatial regions in a visual input. While this core concept is widely shared, the specific terminology varies, including "multimodal grounding," "spatial understanding," "phrase grounding," and "referring expression comprehension," applied to diverse visual media such as images, 3D scenes, videos, and GUIs. A much less common usage, found in one paper, frames "rule grounding" as a logical task of instantiating abstract rules with constants. The field most frequently operationalizes this construct through tasks requiring the localization of objects, which are measured by benchmarks like RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and RefCOCOg. The expected model behavior is to produce a spatial reference, such as generating bounding box coordinates or segmentation masks, with one study noting that models are "required to explicitly ground objects in images by generating coordinates for them" (Verifiable by Design: Aligning Language Models to Quote from Pre-Training Data). Visual grounding is often discussed in contrast to hallucination, with multiple papers describing hallucinations as responses that are "plausible yet visually ungrounded" (The Hidden Life of Tokens: Reducing Hallucination of Large Vision-Language Models Via Visual Information Steering). The construct is reported to influence capabilities like robotic manipulation and visual question answering and is studied alongside related tasks such as object detection and semantic segmentation.
